revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Kamo Shrine]]

revision.comment: initial text of a start of an article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} '''Shimogamo Shrine''' is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/02/ Shimogamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}}.
Shimogamo jinja is amongst the oldest in Japan and it is featured prominently amongst "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]" which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]].<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Kamo-Tamayori-hime]], who is the mother of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]]. Shimogamo is also dedicated to [[Kamo-Taketsune]], who is the father of Kamo-Taayori-hime. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan, including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|Romon File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
{{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-706-81632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-706-81632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82259-0 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449]
==External links== * [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html Shimogamo Shrine website]

(2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}}.
Shimogamo jinja is amongst the oldest in Japan and it is featured prominently amongst "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]" which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]].<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Kamo-Tamayori-hime]], who is the mother of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]]. Shimogamo is also dedicated to [[Kamo-Taketsune]], who is the father of Kamo-Taayori-hime. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan, including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|Romon File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] {{nihongo|'''Shimogamo Shrine'''|下鴨神社|''Shimogamo jinja''}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary on the banks of the Kamo River in northeast Kyoto.<ref>Kyoto Prefectural Government Tourism Division: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/02/ Shimogamo;] Iwao, Seiichi ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&pg=PA1704&lpg=PA1712&dq=clan+kyogoku&source=bl&ots=nda9tD2GRu&sig=YEoEzzqLEf9Wr9IZc8yLzQEbNWk&hl=en&ei=G_tZSpfqDNWktwe_tLjdCg&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=5 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 1712.]</ref> Its formal name is the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}}.
Shimogamo jinja is amongst the oldest in Japan and it is featured prominently amongst "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]" which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]].<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Kamo-Tamayori-hime]], who is the mother of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]]. Shimogamo is also dedicated to [[Kamo-Taketsune]], who is the father of Kamo-Taayori-hime. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|Romon File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-706-81632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-706-81632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82259-0 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449]
==External links== * [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html Shimogamo Shrine website]

In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Kamo-Tamayori-hime]], who is the mother of [[Kamo Wake-ikazuchi]]. Shimogamo is also dedicated to [[Kamo-Taketsune]], who is the father of Kamo-Taayori-hime. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社). It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto.
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>

revision.comment: general editing, clean-up, addition of kanji, and some expansion especially to include the connection with Kamigamo Shrine.
revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社). It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto.
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|Romon File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社). It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo jinja serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). {{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Structures=== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|Romon File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.

In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

revision.comment: robot Adding: [[ko:가모미오야 신사]], [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]]
revision.text:
{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|Romon File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1964). [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}}

(2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

[[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.<ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

[[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto.<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.<ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Shimogamo shrine, along with the Kamigamo shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto Shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.<ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto;<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see [[Heian-kyō]]). The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine, along with the Kamigamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto Shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Shimogamo Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.<ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.

File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

{{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{coord|35|03|37|N|135|45|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:7th-century architecture]] [[Category:Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]]
<!-- Page in other languages --> [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[fr:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto;<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see [[Heian-kyō]]). The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Shimogamo, along with the Kamigamo, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.<ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/>. * '''979''' (''[[Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>

(1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}}

<!-- Page in other languages --> [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[fr:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]]

{{Main|Kamo Shrine}} [[File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|thumb|right|260px|Front view of ''Shimogamo-jinja.'']] '''Shimogamo Shrine''', called Shimogamo-''jinja'' (下鴨神社) in Japanese, is the common name of an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of [[Kyoto]] city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is Kamo-mioya-jinja (賀茂御祖神社).<ref>Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118.</ref> It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]] which have been designated by [[UNESCO]] as a [[World Heritage Site]]. The term Kamo-''jinja'' in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked Kamo shrines of Kyoto;<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.]</ref> Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see [[Heian-kyō]]). The Kamo''-jinja'' serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.<ref>Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of Tamayori-hime (玉依姫; lit., the spirit-inviting maiden) and her father, Kamo Taketsunomi (賀茂建角身). Tamayori-hime is the mother of Kamo Wakeikazuchi (賀茂別雷; the thunder-divider of Kamo), who was sired by Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto (火雷神; the God of Fire and Thunder).<ref>''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.</ref><ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime].</ref> Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref>
Shimogamo, along with the Kamigamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamashiro province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010</ref>
In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.<ref name="ponsonby124">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''[[Enryaku]] 13''): [[Emperor Kammu]] came as part of a grand progress.<ref name="visiting47">Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan,'' p. 47.</ref> * '''942''' (''[[Tengyō]] 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Suzaku]] visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace.<ref name="visiting47"/> * '''979''' (''[[Tengen (era)|Tengen]] 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): [[Emperor En'yū]] decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.<ref name="visiting48">Ponsonby-Fane, Visiting,'' p. 48.</ref> * '''1088''' (''[[Kanji]] 27th day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Horikawa]] visited Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49.</ref> * '''1156''' (''[[Hōgen (era)|Hōgen]] 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): [[Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] traveled to Kamo.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 50.</ref>
==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the [[Emperor Tenmu]], and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to [[Heian-kyo]].<ref>[http://www.japaneselifestyle.com.au/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm Shimgamo Shrine]</ref>
Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century.
===Gallery=== <gallery> File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|[[Rōmon]] File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

(1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1964).
