(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} {{nihongo|'''Shimogamo Shrine'''|下鴨神社|Shimogamo-jinja|lead=yes}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is {{nihongo|'''Kamo-mioya Shrine'''|賀茂御祖神社|Kamo-mioya-jinja}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}} which have been designated by {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}. The term [[Kamo shrines|Kamo-''jinja'']] in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]]s of Kyoto;Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}}). The Kamo-jinja serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of {{nihongo|Tamayori-hime|玉依姫|extra=lit. 'the spirit-inviting maiden'}} and her father, {{nihongo|Kamo Taketsunomi|賀茂建角身}}. Tamayori-hime is the mother of {{nihongo|Kamo Wakeikazuchi|賀茂別雷|extra=the thunder-divider of Kamo}}, who was sired by {{nihongo|Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto|火雷神|extra=the God of Fire and Thunder}}.''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime]. Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Shimogamo, along with the Kamigamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies'', pp. 116–117. The writer of {{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki}}, {{nihongo|{{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei}}|鴨長明}},　was the second son of one of the head priests of the shrine, {{nihongo|Kamo no Nagatsugu|鴨長継}}.Hare, Thomas Blenman. (1989). ''Reading Kamo no Choumei'', p. 174. From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. Today, it is one of the most visited sites during the new year, and the popular national pastime game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari}} is often played by Shinto priests. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''{{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku}} 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', p. 47. * '''942''' (''{{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō}} 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku}} visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''{{ill|Tengen (era)|en|Tengen (era)|WD=Q1327101|lt=Tengen}} 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): {{ill|Emperor En'yū|en|Emperor En'yū|WD=Q374104|lt=Emperor En'yū}} decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 48. * '''1088''' (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}} 27th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|WD=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa}} visited Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49. * '''1156''' (''{{ill|Hōgen (era)|en|Hōgen (era)|WD=Q1193898|lt=Hōgen}} 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} traveled to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 50. ==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}, and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |title=Shimgamo Shrine |access-date=2012-04-24 |archive-date=2015-01-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104163318/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |url-status=dead }} Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century. A model of the Hut described in ''{{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki}}'' is located in Kawai Jinja section of the shrine.{{Cite web|url=http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2012/01/12/celebrating-chomei-shimogamo-shrine/|title=Celebrating Chomei (Shimogamo Shrine) |website=www.greenshinto.com|first=John|last=D|date=12 January 2012 }} ===Gallery=== File:Maples leaves and lantern.jpg|Maple leaves and paper lantern at the shrine File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}} File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] * {{ill|Mitarashi dango|en|Mitarashi dango|WD=Q6880780|lt=Mitarashi dango}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== * {{Commonscat-inline}} * [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp Shimogamo Shrine website] {{in lang|ja}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Shimogamo]] {{Commons category|Shimogamo-jinja}} [[eo:Sanktejo Ŝimogamo]] [[es:Santuario Shimogamo]] [[eu:Shimogamo santutegia]] [[fa:معبد شیموگامو]] [[fi:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[fr:Shimogamo-jinja (Kyoto)]] [[id:Kuil Shimogamo]] [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[la:Templum Simogamo]] [[my:ရှိမိုဂမိုနတ်ကျောင်း]] [[pl:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[pt:Kamomioya-jinja]] [[ru:Симогамо-дзиндзя]] [[simple:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[sl:Svetišče Šimogamo]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าชิโมงาโมะ]] [[tr:Shimogamo Tapınağı]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]] {{wikidata link|Q701620}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 6th century]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606|1=Kamo shrines}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Shimogamo Shrine'''|下鴨神社|Shimogamo-jinja|lead=yes}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is {{nihongo|'''Kamo-mioya Shrine'''|賀茂御祖神社|Kamo-mioya-jinja}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. Today, it is one of the most visited sites during the new year, and the popular national pastime game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} is often played by Shinto priests. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''{{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', p. 47. * '''942''' (''{{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''{{ill|Tengen (era)|en|Tengen (era)|WD=Q1327101|lt=Tengen|1=Tengen (era)}} 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): {{ill|Emperor En'yū|en|Emperor En'yū|WD=Q374104|lt=Emperor En'yū|1=Emperor En'yū}} decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 48. * '''1088''' (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} 27th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|WD=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa|1=Emperor Horikawa}} visited Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49. * '''1156''' (''{{ill|Hōgen (era)|en|Hōgen (era)|WD=Q1193898|lt=Hōgen|1=Hōgen (era)}} 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} traveled to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 50. ==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |title=Shimgamo Shrine |access-date=2012-04-24 |archive-date=2015-01-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104163318/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |url-status=dead }} Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century. A model of the Hut described in ''{{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}'' is located in Kawai Jinja section of the shrine.{{Cite web|url=http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2012/01/12/celebrating-chomei-shimogamo-shrine/|title=Celebrating Chomei (Shimogamo Shrine) |website=www.greenshinto.com|first=John|last=D|date=12 January 2012 }} ===Gallery=== File:Maples leaves and lantern.jpg|Maple leaves and paper lantern at the shrine File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). [[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Taketsunomi|WD=Q11634939|1=Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|WD=Q11572681|1=Tamakushi-hime}} | founded_by = | established = 6th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Shimogamo Izumigawa-chō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | website = {{URL|http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606|1=Kamo shrines}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Shimogamo Shrine'''|下鴨神社|Shimogamo-jinja|lead=yes}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is {{nihongo|'''Kamo-mioya Shrine'''|賀茂御祖神社|Kamo-mioya-jinja}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}} which have been designated by {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}. The term [[Kamo shrines|Kamo-''jinja'']] in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]]s of Kyoto;Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}). The Kamo-jinja serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of {{nihongo|Tamayori-hime|玉依姫|extra=lit. 'the spirit-inviting maiden'}} and her father, {{nihongo|Kamo Taketsunomi|賀茂建角身}}. Tamayori-hime is the mother of {{nihongo|Kamo Wakeikazuchi|賀茂別雷|extra=the thunder-divider of Kamo}}, who was sired by {{nihongo|Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto|火雷神|extra=the God of Fire and Thunder}}.''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime]. Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Shimogamo, along with the Kamigamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies'', pp. 116–117. The writer of {{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}, {{nihongo|{{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei|1=Kamo no Chōmei}}|鴨長明}},　was the second son of one of the head priests of the shrine, {{nihongo|Kamo no Nagatsugu|鴨長継}}.Hare, Thomas Blenman. (1989). ''Reading Kamo no Choumei'', p. 174. From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. Today, it is one of the most visited sites during the new year, and the popular national pastime game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} is often played by Shinto priests. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''{{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', p. 47. * '''942''' (''{{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''{{ill|Tengen (era)|en|Tengen (era)|WD=Q1327101|lt=Tengen|1=Tengen (era)}} 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): {{ill|Emperor En'yū|en|Emperor En'yū|WD=Q374104|lt=Emperor En'yū|1=Emperor En'yū}} decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 48. * '''1088''' (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} 27th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|WD=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa|1=Emperor Horikawa}} visited Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49. * '''1156''' (''{{ill|Hōgen (era)|en|Hōgen (era)|WD=Q1193898|lt=Hōgen|1=Hōgen (era)}} 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} traveled to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 50. ==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |title=Shimgamo Shrine |access-date=2012-04-24 |archive-date=2015-01-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104163318/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |url-status=dead }} Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century. A model of the Hut described in ''{{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}'' is located in Kawai Jinja section of the shrine.{{Cite web|url=http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2012/01/12/celebrating-chomei-shimogamo-shrine/|title=Celebrating Chomei (Shimogamo Shrine) |website=www.greenshinto.com|first=John|last=D|date=12 January 2012 }} ===Gallery=== File:Maples leaves and lantern.jpg|Maple leaves and paper lantern at the shrine File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). 下鴨神社 | image = File:Shimogamo 01.jpg | caption = Shimogamo Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.03888889|N|135.7725|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Shikinaisya
Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}}
Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Taketsunomi|WD=Q11634939|1=Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|WD=Q11572681|1=Tamakushi-hime}} | founded_by = | established = 6th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Shimogamo Izumigawa-chō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | website = {{URL|http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606|1=Kamo shrines}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Shimogamo Shrine'''|下鴨神社|Shimogamo-jinja|lead=yes}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is {{nihongo|'''Kamo-mioya Shrine'''|賀茂御祖神社|Kamo-mioya-jinja}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}} which have been designated by {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}. The term [[Kamo shrines|Kamo-''jinja'']] in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]]s of Kyoto;Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}). The Kamo-jinja serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of {{nihongo|Tamayori-hime|玉依姫|extra=lit. 'the spirit-inviting maiden'}} and her father, {{nihongo|Kamo Taketsunomi|賀茂建角身}}. Tamayori-hime is the mother of {{nihongo|Kamo Wakeikazuchi|賀茂別雷|extra=the thunder-divider of Kamo}}, who was sired by {{nihongo|Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto|火雷神|extra=the God of Fire and Thunder}}.''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime]. Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Shimogamo, along with the Kamigamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies'', pp. 116–117. The writer of {{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}, {{nihongo|{{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei|1=Kamo no Chōmei}}|鴨長明}},　was the second son of one of the head priests of the shrine, {{nihongo|Kamo no Nagatsugu|鴨長継}}.Hare, Thomas Blenman. (1989). ''Reading Kamo no Choumei'', p. 174. From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. Today, it is one of the most visited sites during the new year, and the popular national pastime game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} is often played by Shinto priests. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''{{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', p. 47. * '''942''' (''{{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''{{ill|Tengen (era)|en|Tengen (era)|WD=Q1327101|lt=Tengen|1=Tengen (era)}} 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): {{ill|Emperor En'yū|en|Emperor En'yū|WD=Q374104|lt=Emperor En'yū|1=Emperor En'yū}} decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 48. * '''1088''' (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} 27th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|WD=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa|1=Emperor Horikawa}} visited Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49. * '''1156''' (''{{ill|Hōgen (era)|en|Hōgen (era)|WD=Q1193898|lt=Hōgen|1=Hōgen (era)}} 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} traveled to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 50. ==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |title=Shimgamo Shrine |access-date=2012-04-24 |archive-date=2015-01-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104163318/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |url-status=dead }} Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century. A model of the Hut described in ''{{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}'' is located in Kawai Jinja section of the shrine.{{Cite web|url=http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2012/01/12/celebrating-chomei-shimogamo-shrine/|title=Celebrating Chomei (Shimogamo Shrine) |website=www.greenshinto.com|first=John|last=D|date=12 January 2012 }} ===Gallery=== File:Maples leaves and lantern.jpg|Maple leaves and paper lantern at the shrine File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}} File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] * {{ill|Mitarashi dango|en|Mitarashi dango|WD=Q6880780|lt=Mitarashi dango|1=Mitarashi dango}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== * {{Commonscat-inline}} * [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp Shimogamo Shrine website] {{in lang|ja}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Shimogamo]] {{Commons category|Shimogamo-jinja}} [[eo:Sanktejo Ŝimogamo]] [[es:Santuario Shimogamo]] [[eu:Shimogamo santutegia]] [[fa:معبد شیموگامو]] [[fi:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[fr:Shimogamo-jinja (Kyoto)]] [[id:Kuil Shimogamo]] [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[la:Templum Simogamo]] [[my:ရှိမိုဂမိုနတ်ကျောင်း]] [[pl:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[pt:Kamomioya-jinja]] [[ru:Симогамо-дзиндзя]] [[simple:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[sl:Svetišče Šimogamo]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าชิโมงาโมะ]] [[tr:Shimogamo Tapınağı]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]] {{wikidata link|Q701620}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 6th century]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[Beppyo Shrine]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Taketsunomi|WD=Q11634939|1=Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|WD=Q11572681|1=Tamakushi-hime}} | founded_by = | established = 6th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Shimogamo Izumigawa-chō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | website = {{URL|http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606|1=Kamo shrines}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Shimogamo Shrine'''|下鴨神社|Shimogamo-jinja|lead=yes}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is {{nihongo|'''Kamo-mioya Shrine'''|賀茂御祖神社|Kamo-mioya-jinja}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}} which have been designated by {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}. The term [[Kamo shrines|Kamo-''jinja'']] in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]]s of Kyoto;Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}). The Kamo-jinja serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of {{nihongo|Tamayori-hime|玉依姫|extra=lit. 'the spirit-inviting maiden'}} and her father, {{nihongo|Kamo Taketsunomi|賀茂建角身}}. Tamayori-hime is the mother of {{nihongo|Kamo Wakeikazuchi|賀茂別雷|extra=the thunder-divider of Kamo}}, who was sired by {{nihongo|Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto|火雷神|extra=the God of Fire and Thunder}}.''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime]. Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Shimogamo, along with the Kamigamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies'', pp. 116–117. The writer of {{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}, {{nihongo|{{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei|1=Kamo no Chōmei}}|鴨長明}},　was the second son of one of the head priests of the shrine, {{nihongo|Kamo no Nagatsugu|鴨長継}}.Hare, Thomas Blenman. (1989). ''Reading Kamo no Choumei'', p. 174. From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. Today, it is one of the most visited sites during the new year, and the popular national pastime game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} is often played by Shinto priests. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''{{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', p. 47. * '''942''' (''{{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''{{ill|Tengen (era)|en|Tengen (era)|WD=Q1327101|lt=Tengen|1=Tengen (era)}} 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): {{ill|Emperor En'yū|en|Emperor En'yū|WD=Q374104|lt=Emperor En'yū|1=Emperor En'yū}} decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 48. * '''1088''' (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} 27th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|WD=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa|1=Emperor Horikawa}} visited Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49. * '''1156''' (''{{ill|Hōgen (era)|en|Hōgen (era)|WD=Q1193898|lt=Hōgen|1=Hōgen (era)}} 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} traveled to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 50. ==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |title=Shimgamo Shrine |access-date=2012-04-24 |archive-date=2015-01-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104163318/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |url-status=dead }} Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century. A model of the Hut described in ''{{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}'' is located in Kawai Jinja section of the shrine.{{Cite web|url=http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2012/01/12/celebrating-chomei-shimogamo-shrine/|title=Celebrating Chomei (Shimogamo Shrine) |website=www.greenshinto.com|first=John|last=D|date=12 January 2012 }} ===Gallery=== File:Maples leaves and lantern.jpg|Maple leaves and paper lantern at the shrine File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}} File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] * {{ill|Mitarashi dango|en|Mitarashi dango|WD=Q6880780|lt=Mitarashi dango|1=Mitarashi dango}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== * {{Commonscat-inline}} * [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp Shimogamo Shrine website] {{in lang|ja}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Shimogamo]] {{Commons category|Shimogamo-jinja}} [[eo:Sanktejo Ŝimogamo]] [[es:Santuario Shimogamo]] [[eu:Shimogamo santutegia]] [[fa:معبد شیموگامو]] [[fi:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[fr:Shimogamo-jinja (Kyoto)]] [[id:Kuil Shimogamo]] [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[la:Templum Simogamo]] [[my:ရှိမိုဂမိုနတ်ကျောင်း]] [[pl:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[pt:Kamomioya-jinja]] [[ru:Симогамо-дзиндзя]] [[simple:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[sl:Svetišče Šimogamo]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าชิโมงาโมะ]] [[tr:Shimogamo Tapınağı]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]] {{wikidata link|Q701620}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 6th century]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

The writer of {{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}, {{nihongo|{{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei|1=Kamo no Chōmei}}|鴨長明}},　was the second son of one of the head priests of the shrine, {{nihongo|Kamo no Nagatsugu|鴨長継}}.Hare, Thomas Blenman. (1989). ''Reading Kamo no Choumei'', p. 174. From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. Today, it is one of the most visited sites during the new year, and the popular national pastime game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} is often played by Shinto priests. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''{{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', p. 47. * '''942''' (''{{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''{{ill|Tengen (era)|en|Tengen (era)|WD=Q1327101|lt=Tengen|1=Tengen (era)}} 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): {{ill|Emperor En'yū|en|Emperor En'yū|WD=Q374104|lt=Emperor En'yū|1=Emperor En'yū}} decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 48. * '''1088''' (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} 27th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|WD=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa|1=Emperor Horikawa}} visited Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49. * '''1156''' (''{{ill|Hōgen (era)|en|Hōgen (era)|WD=Q1193898|lt=Hōgen|1=Hōgen (era)}} 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} traveled to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 50. ==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |title=Shimgamo Shrine |access-date=2012-04-24 |archive-date=2015-01-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104163318/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |url-status=dead }} Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century. A model of the Hut described in ''{{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}'' is located in Kawai Jinja section of the shrine.{{Cite web|url=http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2012/01/12/celebrating-chomei-shimogamo-shrine/|title=Celebrating Chomei (Shimogamo Shrine) |website=www.greenshinto.com|first=John|last=D|date=12 January 2012 }} ===Gallery=== File:Maples leaves and lantern.jpg|Maple leaves and paper lantern at the shrine File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}} File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] * {{ill|Mitarashi dango|en|Mitarashi dango|WD=Q6880780|lt=Mitarashi dango|1=Mitarashi dango}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{refend}} ==External links== * {{Commonscat-inline}} * [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp Shimogamo Shrine website] {{in lang|ja}} {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Shimogamo]] {{Commons category|Shimogamo-jinja}} [[eo:Sanktejo Ŝimogamo]] [[es:Santuario Shimogamo]] [[eu:Shimogamo santutegia]] [[fa:معبد شیموگامو]] [[fi:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[fr:Shimogamo-jinja (Kyoto)]] [[id:Kuil Shimogamo]] [[ja:賀茂御祖神社]] [[ko:가모미오야 신사]] [[la:Templum Simogamo]] [[my:ရှိမိုဂမိုနတ်ကျောင်း]] [[pl:Shimogamo-jinja]] [[pt:Kamomioya-jinja]] [[ru:Симогамо-дзиндзя]] [[simple:Shimogamo Shrine]] [[sl:Svetišče Šimogamo]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าชิโมงาโมะ]] [[tr:Shimogamo Tapınağı]] [[zh:賀茂御祖神社]] {{wikidata link|Q701620}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 6th century]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

下鴨神社 | image = File:Shimogamo 01.jpg | caption = Shimogamo Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.03888889|N|135.7725|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Shikinaisya
Yamashiro no Kuni ichinomiya
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}}
Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpeitaisha]]
[[Chokusaisha]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] | deity = {{ill|Kamo Taketsunomi|WD=Q11634939|1=Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|WD=Q11572681|1=Tamakushi-hime}} | founded_by = | established = 6th century | date_destroyed = | location = 59 Shimogamo Izumigawa-chō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | website = {{URL|http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | festival = [[Aoi Matsuri]]
(Kamo no Matsuri; May 15th) | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} | part_of= {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Kamo Shrine|WD=Q1306606|1=Kamo shrines}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Shimogamo Shrine'''|下鴨神社|Shimogamo-jinja|lead=yes}} is an important [[Shinto]] sanctuary in the Shimogamo district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} city's Sakyō ward. Its formal name is {{nihongo|'''Kamo-mioya Shrine'''|賀茂御祖神社|Kamo-mioya-jinja}}.Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 1-118. It is one of the oldest Shinto shrines in Japan and is one of the seventeen {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}} which have been designated by {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}. The term [[Kamo shrines|Kamo-''jinja'']] in Japanese is a general reference to Shimogamo Shrine and [[Kamigamo Shrine]], the traditionally linked [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]]s of Kyoto;Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire'', p. 479.] Shimogamo is the older of the pair, being believed to be 100 years older than Kamigamo, and dating to the 6th century, centuries before Kyoto became the capital of Japan (794, see {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}). The Kamo-jinja serve the function of protecting Kyoto from malign influences.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110321000139/http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm |date=2011-03-21 }} ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The ''jinja'' name identifies the Kamo family of ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated. The name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods, which are vestiges of the primeval forest of [[Tadasu no Mori]]. In addition, the shrine name references the area's early inhabitants, the [[Kamo clan]], many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] Shimogamo Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of {{nihongo|Tamayori-hime|玉依姫|extra=lit. 'the spirit-inviting maiden'}} and her father, {{nihongo|Kamo Taketsunomi|賀茂建角身}}. Tamayori-hime is the mother of {{nihongo|Kamo Wakeikazuchi|賀茂別雷|extra=the thunder-divider of Kamo}}, who was sired by {{nihongo|Honoikazuchi-no-mikoto|火雷神|extra=the God of Fire and Thunder}}.''Kōjien'' Japanese dictionary, entry for Tamayori-bime.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=152 Kokugakuin University's digital Encyclopedia of Shinto, entry for Tamayorihime]. Kamigamo Shrine, the other of the two Kamo shrines of Kyoto, is dedicated to Kamo Wakeikazuchi. These ''kami'' are variously associated with thunder. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', pp. 74–75.] Shimogamo, along with the Kamigamo Shrine, was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to Japan's guardian ''kami'', including Kamo-Tamayori-hime and Kamo-Taketsune.Ponsonby-Fane. ''Studies'', pp. 116–117. The writer of {{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}, {{nihongo|{{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei|1=Kamo no Chōmei}}|鴨長明}},　was the second son of one of the head priests of the shrine, {{nihongo|Kamo no Nagatsugu|鴨長継}}.Hare, Thomas Blenman. (1989). ''Reading Kamo no Choumei'', p. 174. From 1871 through 1946, Shimogamo was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 124. Today, it is one of the most visited sites during the new year, and the popular national pastime game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} is often played by Shinto priests. ===Imperial visits=== * '''794''' (''{{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 13''): [[Emperor Kanmu]] came as part of a grand progress.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines of Japan'', p. 47. * '''942''' (''{{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} 5, 29th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} visited to offer thanks for restoration of peace. * '''979''' (''{{ill|Tengen (era)|en|Tengen (era)|WD=Q1327101|lt=Tengen|1=Tengen (era)}} 2, 10th day of the 10th month''): {{ill|Emperor En'yū|en|Emperor En'yū|WD=Q374104|lt=Emperor En'yū|1=Emperor En'yū}} decided that an Imperial visit Hachiman at Iwashimizu Shrine should be paired with a visit to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 48. * '''1088''' (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} 27th day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|WD=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa|1=Emperor Horikawa}} visited Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting,'' p. 49. * '''1156''' (''{{ill|Hōgen (era)|en|Hōgen (era)|WD=Q1193898|lt=Hōgen|1=Hōgen (era)}} 1, 23rd day of the 4th month''): {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} traveled to Kamo.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting'', p. 50. ==Structures== A shrine structure was erected in 678 during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and this became the principal building during the reign or of the [[Emperor Kanmu]] when he removed the capital from Nara to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}.{{Cite web |url=http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |title=Shimgamo Shrine |access-date=2012-04-24 |archive-date=2015-01-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104163318/http://www.gojapango.com/travel/kyoto_shimogamo_shrine.htm |url-status=dead }} Shimogamo Shrine (Lower Kamo Shrine) is believed to date to the 6th century. A model of the Hut described in ''{{ill|Hōjōki|en|Hōjōki|WD=Q636364|lt=Hōjōki|1=Hōjōki}}'' is located in Kawai Jinja section of the shrine.{{Cite web|url=http://www.greenshinto.com/wp/2012/01/12/celebrating-chomei-shimogamo-shrine/|title=Celebrating Chomei (Shimogamo Shrine) |website=www.greenshinto.com|first=John|last=D|date=12 January 2012 }} ===Gallery=== File:Maples leaves and lantern.jpg|Maple leaves and paper lantern at the shrine File:Tadasu-no-Mori.jpg|This pathway leads through ''Tadasu no Mori'' (the "Forest Where Lies are Revealed"). File:Shimogamo 01.jpg|{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}} File:Shimogamo Jinja no Torii.jpg|A pair of torii gates, Kawai-jinja File:Shimogamo Jinja Mitarasi sha2.JPG|Mitarai-sha File:Bridge at Shimogamo Shrine.jpg|Taikobashi bridge ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] * {{ill|Mitarashi dango|en|Mitarashi dango|WD=Q6880780|lt=Mitarashi dango|1=Mitarashi dango}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}.
